exemplar.title: To what extent do varying levels of education affect a person's Environmental Value Systems (EVS) and their perceptions and behaviors towards sustainability?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do varying levels of education affect a person's Environmental Value Systems (EVS) and their perceptions and behaviors towards sustainability?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report follows IB IA structure and uses ESS terminology correctly.
Sections are generally easy to locate.
weaknesses: Organisation breaks down with lengthy rawâdata tables in the main body.
Narrative is verbose with redundant wording; unexplained symbols and inconsistent citation style interrupt coherence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and use of terminology are competent, but coherence is hindered by formatting issues, verbosity and occasional errors. Performance is solidly midâband, justifying 2/3.

strengths: Formulates a precise, coherent and focused research question specifying population, variables and relationship.
Clearly identifies EVS categories and sustainability behaviour in the context of education levels.
weaknesses: Discussion of the environmental issue lacks depth and specific impacts to ground the study.
Connections between EVS and sustainability behaviour are described only partially; cultural and socioeconomic mediators are not fully integrated.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The research question fulfils the topâband descriptor (focused, coherent and relevant). However, the contextual discussion of the environmental issue and the linkage between EVS and sustainability behaviour have omissions. Overall performance sits in the midâhigh range of the band, justifying 4/6.

strengths: Survey procedure and chiâsquared testing are described sufficiently for replication.
Method is appropriate to collect relevant data on EVS and sustainability behaviour.
weaknesses: Details on survey validation, question development and piloting are missing.
Convenience sampling is stated but lacks justification regarding validity and generalizability.
Ethical considerations omit informed consent procedures and data protection safeguards.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method design meets the repeatability requirement but lacks critical details (validation, Î± justification). Sampling and ethics are outlined but not justified comprehensively. Overall performance corresponds to a solid midâband level, meriting 3/6.

strengths: Evaluates how results address the research question and recognises ambiguities.
Identifies key strengths and weaknesses of the survey method.
Proposes meaningful study improvements (larger sample, mixedâmethods).
weaknesses: Evaluation omits broader environmental/policy implications of findings.
Limitation of chiâsquare assumptions and sampling bias are not fully explored.
Suggestions are generic and lack detailed methodological guidance.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands perform at a midâband level: the student evaluates conclusions and methods and suggests extensions but with notable omissions and lack of depth. This warrants 3/6.

strengths: Appropriate tables and bar charts present processed data.
Chiâsquared tests are executed correctly with identification of trends.
Conclusions link numerical findings to hypotheses with supporting percentages and pâvalues.
weaknesses: Rawâdata tables are cluttered, some totals and a referenced table are missing.
Analysis omits effect size or confidence intervals and does not justify the unconventional Î±=0.15.
Conclusion occasionally overstates strength of correlations and cuts off midâsentence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation has minor omissions (band 3), while data analysis and conclusion are largely correct but incomplete (band 4). The overall quality aligns with the 3â4 band, closer to the stronger end, justifying 4/6.

strengths: Application proposal is directly derived from findings and justified with reference to emission data.
Considers some strengths and weaknesses of the proposed solution.
weaknesses: Evaluation omits key factors such as curriculum time, teacher workload and resource constraints.
Feasibility analysis lacks detailed budget, stakeholder or implementation planning.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The application is wellâjustified (band 3) but the solution evaluation considers only some relevant factors (band 2). Overall performance aligns with the midâband, yielding 2/3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key ESS terms (EVS, technocentrism, chi-square) are used correctly, yet narrative is verbose and contains redundant wording, showing either good terminology or concision, not both.

The student discusses how results answer the research question and recognises ambiguities, but does not fully relate findings to wider environmental implications (e.g., policy impact).  This is an evaluation with omissions.

The conclusion restates the main numerical findings and links them to the hypotheses, with supporting percentages and p-values.  Some over-generalisations occur (e.g., âstrongly correlatedâ with p=0.134), but overall the trends are interpreted and backed by data, satisfying âsome validityâ and edging toward the top of the band.

Percentages and three chi-squared tests are executed correctly and trends are identified, but there is no check of expected counts or discussion of effect sizes; the unusual Î±=0.15 is not statistically justified.  Analysis is largely correct but incomplete.

Two concrete improvements (larger sample, mixed-methods data collection) and an extension study are suggested and clearly linked to weaknesses.  They are meaningful but not substantial enough to address all major flaws; hence mid-band.

The survey procedure, point-scoring system and chi-squared tests are described sufficiently for another student to replicate, and they can yield relevant data.  Nevertheless, details such as exact survey questions for all variables and how EVS categories are operationalised are incomplete, preventing the highest band.

Connections between education, EVS and sustainable behaviour are described and linked to the research aim, but the explanation remains partial (e.g., cultural factors, limitations of surveys are not integrated).  This fits a description with some omissions rather than a comprehensive explanation.

The student formulates a single, precise research question that clearly identifies the population (people with different education levels), the variables (EVS category and sustainability behaviour) and the relationship to be explored.  The wording is specific enough to guide the investigation and avoids unnecessary breadth, fulfilling the descriptor of a relevant, coherent and focused question.

Strengths (emission reduction) and weaknesses (cost, resistance) are considered, but not all relevant facets (e.g., curriculum time, teacher workload) are evaluated.  This matches âevaluates some strengths and weaknessesâ.

The report follows the IB IA structure and sections are easy to locate, but internal organisation is occasionally unclear (e.g., long raw-data table in body), so structure is present but not sustained flawlessly.

A table lists strengths and weaknesses of the survey method and mentions bias and sample size, but fails to explore limitations of the chi-square assumptions or convenience sampling thoroughly.  Thus the description is present yet not exhaustive.

The submission outlines why EVS and sustainability matter and cites sources showing educationâs growing role, thus giving some context.  However, the environmental issue (societal response to climate change / sustainability) is described only briefly and lacks depth about specific environmental impacts, so the discussion is not thorough enough for the top band.

The student states convenience sampling of 25 participants per education group and explains it was chosen for ease, but provides limited justification of how this affects validity.  The description, while present, lacks a critical rationale, placing it in the 3-4 band.

Basic physical risks (eye strain, back pain) and two ethical points (anonymity, exclusion of minors) are outlined, yet the discussion is brief and does not cover data protection law or consent procedures in depth.  This meets an outline but not a comprehensive description.

Relevant tables and two bar charts are provided for all processed data, yet raw-data tables are cluttered and difficult to interpret and some totals are missing, indicating omissions.  Hence âappropriate with some omissionsâ best fits.

The proposal to make sustainability education mandatory is directly derived from the findings and is justified with reference to observed trends and UN emission-reduction data, satisfying the top-band requirement of a justified application.

The argument generally flows, but large data blocks, occasional grammatical slips and unexplained symbols (e.g., Ï) interrupt coherence, making some sections hard to follow.  Hence it is âmainly logical but difficult in partsâ.

annotations.comment: The research question is precise, clearly identifying the population, variables (EVS vs. sustainability behavior), and their relationship, fulfilling the descriptor for a focused, coherent question.

The introduction of EVS defines categories but lacks linkage to a specific environmental issue (e.g., biodiversity loss), limiting context depth. Consider adding concrete impacts to ground your study.
Connections between cultural factors and EVS are mentioned but not integrated in your rationale. Elaborate on how culture or socioeconomics could mediate educationâs effect on values.
Hypothesis 2 is incomplete and breaks mid-sentence. Ensure each hypothesis is fully articulated and connected explicitly to your research question.
The sampling strategy states convenience sampling but offers little justification of its impact on validity. You should justify this choice and discuss how it affects generalizability.
The method design describes survey scoring but doesnât indicate how questions were validated or piloted. Include details on question development and reliability checks.
Choosing Î± = 0.15 is unconventional and unsupported. Standard practice is Î± = 0.05; if using 0.15, provide a clear rationale linked to exploratory analysis or small sample size.
Ethical considerations omit discussion of consent procedures and data protection (e.g., GDPR compliance). Describe informed consent and confidentiality safeguards in detail.
The raw data table is overly complex and cluttered, making interpretation difficult. Consider moving full raw data to an appendix and summarizing key findings here.
Unexplained symbols (e.g., Ï) appear in the raw data table, which may confuse readers. Define every symbol in a legend or footnote for clarity.
Data processing Table 2 omits totals for certain categories (blank CV column). Complete all cells or note when data are not applicable to ensure accuracy.
Table 3 caption is in the text but the table is missing. Ensure every caption references an existing table or move the caption appropriately.
Chi-squared tables list H0 and H1 but omit brief context on what rejecting H0 implies for your environmental hypothesis. Add a sentence interpreting each result in context.
Figures lack axis labels, units, and data values on bars. Add clear axis titles, percentage values, and a legend to improve readability and interpretation.
Analysis focuses on p-values but omits effect size or confidence intervals. Discuss magnitude of relationships to give a fuller picture beyond statistical significance.
The conclusion section cuts off mid-sentence and repeats earlier text. Ensure a concise concluding paragraph fully synthesizes findings and addresses the research question.
Claiming a âstrongâ connection with p = 0.148 overstates the evidence. Use more cautious language (e.g., âmoderateâ or âweakâ correlation) to accurately reflect the p-value.
The evaluation identifies only one weakness and one improvement. Expand this section to discuss additional limitations (e.g., survey bias, chi-square assumptions) for a balanced critique.
Suggested improvements are generic. Provide precise recommendations, such as a stratified random sampling plan or mixed-methods interviews to strengthen validity.
Future studies propose comparing curricula but lack methodological detail (sample size, instruments). Outline a brief research design to illustrate feasibility and rigor.
The application section recommends mandatory sustainability lessons but doesnât address teacher workload or curriculum time constraints. Evaluate these trade-offs to strengthen justification.
Budget and resistance are noted but missing considerations like curriculum standards and resource availability. Add these to fully evaluate solution feasibility.
Bibliography entries are inconsistently formatted (some missing italics, brackets). Adopt a single citation style (APA, MLA) and apply it consistently.
Several online sources lack access dates and full author details. Verify and complete all reference entries to meet academic citation standards.
